does: This paper investigates how neural superposition—where individual neurons encode multiple features simultaneously through linear combinations—affects the measurement of representational alignment between neural networks. The authors develop a theoretical framework showing that standard alignment metrics like soft-matching and linear regression produce artificially low similarity scores when two models encode identical features but in different superposition arrangements (different linear combinations). They validate this theory by training sparse autoencoders on toy models to disentangle superposition and demonstrate that alignment scores increase substantially when a model's neurons are replaced with sparse, overcomplete bases that separate out individual features.
connects: This work bridges the superposition hypothesis from mechanistic interpretability with representational alignment research from comparative neuroscience and deep learning. It builds on prior work establishing that neural networks use superposition to compress feature representations, and extends alignment methodology literature that measures feature similarity across networks through techniques like canonical correlation analysis and linear regression-based matching. The paper responds to growing recognition that alignment metrics may be insensitive to certain representation schemes, connecting mechanistic interpretability concerns with the broader problem of understanding whether different neural systems implement similar computations despite using different encodings.
enables: This work opens pathways toward more robust alignment metrics that account for superposition structure, potentially improving how we compare representations across different neural networks, brain regions, and even biological and artificial systems. It suggests that sparse autoencoders could serve as a preprocessing step for alignment analysis, revealing hidden similarities obscured by superposition. The framework enables future research on developing superposition-aware metrics that directly decompose features before comparison, facilitates better understanding of which alignment approaches are most reliable under different encoding schemes, and informs interpretability efforts by clarifying when apparent representational differences may simply reflect different superposition strategies rather than fundamental computational divergence.
